engineering.translation: Abandoning (tyaktvā) entirely (aśeṣataḥ), without a trace, all desires (sarvān kāmān) born of fancy (saṅkalpa-prabhavān). Restraining (viniyamya), with the mind alone (manasā eva), the entire group of senses (indriya-grāmam) on every side (samantataḥ). 6.24 names the practice of gradual restraint. Two clauses run in parallel here. The first: tyāga of saṅkalpa-prabhavān desires — desires born of one's own mental willing. The second: the senses restrained by mind on every side. Shankara: these desires are those produced by mental volitions; the senses are restrained from running toward objects. Ramanuja treats 6.24-25 as a paired unit. Two kinds of desires must be relinquished — those born of sense-contact and those generated by mental willing. The years of compounded practice are operational. STRETCHED at full scope.
engineering.concrete_scenario: An engineer ten years into the practice of writing systems-software has compounded a specific discipline: she does not chase architecture-review attention, conference-talk slots, the most-fashionable framework, the most-quoted-on-Twitter design pattern, the GitHub-star count of her side projects. These are saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmāḥ — desires that arose from her own mental willing about how an engineering career should look. Each was abandoned, not in a single act, but in the slow accumulation of choosing the boring-correct decision over the visible one — the maintainable PostgreSQL schema instead of the speculative graph-database rewrite, the unglamorous monitoring instrumentation instead of the keynote-friendly machine-learning experiment. Ten years of the choices have produced a senior whose attention is no longer pulled by these desires. The mind has restrained the sense-organs (the part of her that responds to status-cues) on every side, samantataḥ. Compare the engineer at year ten who still chases the visible decision; the wind has not been removed at the source. The verse's gradualism is honest: this is not a six-week intensive. STRETCHED at full metaphysical scope; partial engineering analog at compounded-discipline scope.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader reads the verse as 'don't want any career advancement.' The chapter assumes engaged action; the renouncer/yogi equation of 6.1 explicitly rejects withdrawal. Saṅkalpa-prabhavān kāmān names a specific class of desires (those generated by mental willing about what one's career should look like, untethered from the actual work), not the entire space of motivation.
engineering.counter_example: When the engineer's apparent disinterest in promotion or recognition is in fact resentment — they wanted these things and did not get them, and now claim disinterest as defence — the verse does not endorse this. Tyāga is positive abandonment by direct seeing, not the sour-grapes posture of denied wanting.
engineering.implication: Notice which engineering-career desires are saṅkalpa-prabhavān — generated by your own mental willing about how a career 'should look' — versus emergent from the actual work. The first class is what the verse asks you to abandon; the gradual abandonment is the practice.
engineering.quotable_line: Abandon the desires you have generated about how your career should look; the actual work then occupies its own ground.

iterations.mutation: v0 generation: BG 6.24 names gradual-restraint practice. Both clauses (tyāga of saṅkalpa-prabhavān + sense-restraint by mind) preserved. Shankara/Ramanuja-paired-with-6.25 preserved. Counter-example distinguishes tyāga from resentment. Tools: PostgreSQL, GitHub, Twitter. Confidence MEDIUM at compounded-practice scope; STRETCHED at metaphysical scope.
